participants.extracted: Adults (aged 18 years and older) with serious surgical site infection (SSI) occurring more than 30 days after the primary surgery were enrolled at Tianjin Binhai New Area Dagang Hospital in China between May 2005 and June 2012. Eligible patients had failed non-surgical treatment for SSI. Patients were excluded if they were younger than 18 years, had an immune system disorder-related disease, or had a prosthetic implant at the surgical site. A total of 106 participants were randomized: 57 to the endoscopy procedure group and 49 to the traditional open surgery group. Baseline characteristics, including age, sex distribution, body mass index, and type of primary surgery, were similar between groups.

interventions.extracted: Endoscopy procedure for debridement of the infected surgical wound: the surgical field was sterilized conventionally with sterile towels, a small opening of about 6 mm was made along the original incision to allow insertion of a choledochoscope, and the wound was washed with sterile saline. Under direct visualization, necrotic tissue and infected suture material were completely removed; for sinuses too small to accommodate the scope, necrotic tissue was removed with a biopsy clip. Instead of leaving a drainage tube for several days, saline gauze was used for drainage for no longer than 24 hours. Antibiotic prophylaxis was administered intravenously 30 minutes before incision.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure wound healing time as the primary outcome. Secondary outcomes were duration of surgery (minutes), blood loss (ml), pain level 7 days after surgery measured by the visual analog scale (VAS), volume of irrigation saline (ml), rate of skin transplantation, and length of hospital stay (days). The study also reports follow-up for postoperative clinical complications over at least 4 weeks, although this was not listed as a primary or secondary outcome measure.
